1:
"And thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare: and the height thereof shall be three cubits." The witnesses note the making of the altar of burnt offering — the crux of the place of sacrifice. On "thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare," the grammatical voices note the altar. Henry and Gill note that "thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare: and the height thereof shall be three cubits" — the altar of burnt offering (of acacia wood, overlaid with brass, vv.2), foursquare, of the given dimensions, in the court (the great altar where the sacrifices were offered); the altar of burnt offering (the brazen altar, the first object on entering the court, the place of atonement and approach to God).

On "an altar of shittim wood," interpreters expound the altar. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the altar of sacrifice — the place where the burnt offerings and sacrifices were offered (the daily sacrifices, the sin offerings, the burnt offerings, Leviticus 1-7); the witnesses note the figure — the altar of burnt offering a figure of Christ and his cross (the place of sacrifice and atonement, where the offering was made, the blood shed, the wrath appeased); the lesson — Christ both the altar and the sacrifice (Hebrews 13:10, "we have an altar"; the cross the altar, Christ the offering, Ephesians 5:2, "an offering and a sacrifice to God"); the way to God through sacrifice (the sinner approaching God first at the altar, the blood, the atonement, before the laver, the holy place, the holiest; the order of approach, through the cross).

On the dimensions, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the altar: the altar foursquare (the great altar of the court) — the place of the daily sacrifice; the providence (the altar the figure of the cross, the place of atonement, the way to God; the first thing in the court, the sacrifice the ground of all approach, Hebrews 9:22, "without shedding of blood is no remission"). The verse displays the making of the altar of burnt offering.

The witnesses agree the substance: the altar of burnt offering, of acacia overlaid with brass, foursquare, was the place of sacrifice (Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the place of sacrifice: the altar of burnt offering — the place where the sacrifices were offered, the blood shed, the atonement made (Leviticus 1-7), a figure of Christ and his cross (Hebrews 13:10; Ephesians 5:2), the way to God through sacrifice, the first thing in the court, the sacrifice the ground of all approach (Hebrews 9:22). The verse displays the altar-of-sacrifice crux. The verse marks the altar: of burnt offering. The verse displays the sacrifice: offered. The verse displays the atonement: made. The verse displays the Christ: the altar and offering. The altar displays the place of sacrifice, the figure of the cross. The verse displays the altar: foursquare. The verse displays the blood: shed. The verse displays the cross: foreshadowed. The making of the altar of burnt offering displays the crux: an altar of shittim wood, the place of sacrifice (Leviticus 1-7; Hebrews 13:10; Ephesians 5:2; 9:22) — a figure of Christ and his cross, the way to God through the atoning sacrifice. The verse displays the horns of the altar (v.2).
2:
"And thou shalt make the horns of it upon the four corners thereof: his horns shall be of the same: and thou shalt overlay it with brass." The witnesses note the horns of the altar and the overlay of brass — the crux of the horns of refuge. On "thou shalt make the horns of it upon the four corners thereof," interpreters expound the horns. The reformed and the dominant reading note the horns — "the horns of it upon the four corners... his horns shall be of the same": four horns (projections) at the four corners of the altar, of one piece with it; the witnesses note the uses and figure — the horns where the blood of the sin offering was applied (Leviticus 4:7, 18, 25, 30; the atoning blood put on the horns); the horns laid hold of for refuge (the place of asylum, Adonijah and Joab fleeing to "the horns of the altar," 1 Kings 1:50-51; 2:28); the horns to which the sacrifice was bound (Psalm 118:27, "bind the sacrifice with cords, even unto the horns of the altar"); the lesson — the horns of the altar the place of refuge and the application of the blood, a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement (the sinner laying hold on Christ and the horns of his altar, fleeing for refuge to the hope set before us, Hebrews 6:18; the power and salvation of the cross, the "horn of salvation," Luke 1:69); the strength and refuge of the atonement.

On "his horns shall be of the same," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the unity. Wesley and Ellicott note the horns of one piece with the altar (not separate, the horns and the altar one) — the unity (the horns part of the altar, the refuge part of the sacrifice).

On "thou shalt overlay it with brass," the grammatical-historical note the brass: "thou shalt overlay it with brass" — the altar overlaid with brass (bronze, able to bear the fire, the figure, some note, of judgment endured, the brazen altar bearing the fire of God's wrath, as Christ bore the fire of judgment); the providence (the brazen altar, the place of atonement, the fire of judgment borne; a figure of the cross, where Christ endured the fire of wrath). The verse displays the horns of the altar and the overlay of brass.

The witnesses agree the substance: the four horns of the altar were of one piece, the altar overlaid with brass (Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the horns of refuge: the horns of the altar — the place where the atoning blood was applied (Leviticus 4:7) and where the fleeing laid hold for refuge (1 Kings 1:50-51; Psalm 118:27), a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement (Hebrews 6:18; Luke 1:69, the "horn of salvation"); the brass bearing the fire of judgment, a figure of the cross. The verse displays the horns-of-refuge crux. The verse marks the horns: four. The verse displays the blood: applied. The verse displays the refuge: the horns. The verse displays the Christ: the horn of salvation. The horns display the place of refuge and atonement. The verse displays the horns: of one piece. The verse displays the brass: the overlay. The verse displays the Christ: the refuge. The horns of the altar and the overlay of brass display the crux: the horns of the altar, the place of refuge and the blood (Leviticus 4:7; 1 Kings 1:50-51; Psalm 118:27; Hebrews 6:18; Luke 1:69) — a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement, the brass bearing the fire of judgment. The verse displays the vessels of the altar (v.3).
3:
"And thou shalt make his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass." The very brief verse notes the brazen vessels of the altar. The grammatical voices note the vessels. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou shalt make his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass" — the vessels of the altar (pans for the ashes, shovels, basins for the blood, fleshhooks for the flesh, firepans for the coals) were all of brass; the furniture of the altar (the instruments for the service of the sacrifices); the brass (the vessels of the altar, matching the brazen altar, for the service of atonement).

On "his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons," the witnesses note the vessels. Gill notes the pans, shovels, basins (for the ashes, the cleansing, the blood) — the instruments of the altar's service.

On "his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass," the grammatical-historical note the brass: "all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass" — the fleshhooks (for the flesh of the sacrifices) and firepans (for the coals), all of brass; the providence (the vessels of the altar all brass, for the service of the sacrifices, the daily atonement). The verse displays the brazen vessels of the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vessels — "his pans... and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all... of brass," the furniture (Ellicott, Gill); "his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons," the vessels (Gill); "his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass," the brass (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the altar's vessels. The verse marks the pans: for the ashes. The verse displays the basins: for the blood. The verse displays the fleshhooks: and firepans. The verse displays the brass: all. The vessels display the furniture of the altar's service. The verse displays the vessels: brazen. The verse displays the altar: served. The verse displays the sacrifices: tended. The brazen vessels of the altar display the furniture of the altar's service: pans, shovels, basins, fleshhooks, firepans, all of brass — the instruments for the service of the sacrifices, the daily atonement. The verse displays the grate of network (vv.4-5).
4:
"And thou shalt make for it a grate of network of brass; and upon the net shalt thou make four brasen rings in the four corners thereof." The very brief verse notes the brazen grate of the altar. The grammatical voices note the grate. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou shalt make for it a grate of network of brass; and upon the net shalt thou make four brasen rings in the four corners thereof" — a grate (a grating, a network) of brass was made for the altar, with four brazen rings at its corners (for the staves, vv.6-7); the grate (the bronze network, perhaps a hearth or ledge for the fire and the offerings, or a grating in the middle of the altar); the rings (for the staves to bear the altar); the workmanship of the altar.

On "a grate of network of brass," the witnesses note the grate. Gill notes the brazen grate (the network, for the fire and the offerings, set in the altar) — the hearth; the workmanship.

On "four brasen rings in the four corners thereof," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the rings: "four brasen rings in the four corners thereof" — the rings (for the staves, the carrying of the altar, vv.6-7); the provision for bearing (the altar borne by staves); the providence (the altar carried in the journey, the pilgrim sanctuary). The verse displays the brazen grate of the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the grate — "a grate of network of brass; and upon the net... four brasen rings in the four corners thereof," the grate (vv.6-7, Ellicott, Gill); "a grate of network of brass," the grate (Gill); "four brasen rings in the four corners thereof," the rings (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the grated altar. The verse marks the grate: of brass. The verse displays the network: the hearth. The verse displays the rings: four. The verse displays the carrying: provided. The grate displays the workmanship and bearing of the altar. The verse displays the grate: brazen. The verse displays the rings: for staves. The verse displays the altar: to be borne. The brazen grate of the altar displays the workmanship: a grate of brass with four rings (vv.6-7) — the bronze network and the rings for the staves to bear the altar. The verse displays the placing of the grate (v.5).
5:
"And thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar." The very brief verse notes the placing of the grate within the altar. The grammatical voices note the placement. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar" — the grate was placed under the ledge (compass, a projecting rim) of the altar, reaching to the middle of the altar's height; the placement (the grate set within, halfway up the altar, perhaps to hold the fire and the offerings, or as a grating through which the ashes fell); the workmanship (the grate fitted within the altar).

On "under the compass of the altar beneath," the witnesses note the ledge. Gill notes the compass (a ledge, a projecting border around the altar) under which the grate was set — the placement; the structure.

On "that the net may be even to the midst of the altar," the grammatical-historical note the position: "that the net may be even to the midst of the altar" — the grate reaching to the middle (halfway up the altar's height); the workmanship (the grate fitted within); the providence (the altar made as appointed, the grate for the fire and the service). The verse displays the placing of the grate within the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the placement — "thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar," the placement (Keil, Ellicott); "under the compass of the altar beneath," the ledge (Gill); "that the net may be even to the midst of the altar," the position (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the placed grate. The verse marks the grate: under the compass. The verse displays the ledge: the rim. The verse displays the position: the midst. The verse displays the staves of the altar (vv.6-7).
6:
"And thou shalt make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass." The very brief verse notes the staves for bearing the altar. The grammatical voices note the staves. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou shalt make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass" — the staves (poles, of acacia wood overlaid with brass, matching the brazen altar) for carrying the altar; the staves (for the bearing of the altar, through the rings, vv.7); the brass overlay (matching the altar, not gold as the ark's, for the altar was brass); the provision for transport (the altar borne by staves).

On "staves of shittim wood," the witnesses note the poles. Gill notes the staves of acacia (for carrying the altar) — the poles; the bearing.

On "overlay them with brass," the grammatical-historical note the brass: "overlay them with brass" — the brass overlay (matching the altar of the court, the brazen altar); the provision (the staves through the rings, the altar borne, vv.7); the providence (the altar carried in the journey, the pilgrim sanctuary). The verse displays the staves for bearing the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the staves — "make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass," the poles (vv.7, Ellicott, Gill); "staves of shittim wood," the poles (Gill); "overlay them with brass," the brass (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the made staves. The verse marks the staves: of shittim wood. The verse displays the brass: overlaid. The verse displays the altar: to be borne. The verse displays the staves in the rings (v.7).
7:
"And the staves shall be put into the rings, and the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it." The very brief verse notes the staves placed to bear the altar. The grammatical voices note the placement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the staves shall be put into the rings, and the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it" — the staves were put through the rings on the two sides of the altar, that it might be carried; the bearing (the altar borne by the staves, on the journey); the provision for transport (the altar, like the ark and table, carried by staves); the pilgrim sanctuary (the altar ever ready to move with the people).

On "the staves shall be put into the rings," the witnesses note the placement. Gill notes the staves through the rings (the altar made ready to bear) — the provision; the bearing.

On "the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it," the grammatical-historical note the carrying: "the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it" — the staves on the two sides (for the bearing); the altar carried by the staves; the providence (the altar borne in the journey, the furniture carried by the Levites, Numbers 4:13-14). The verse displays the staves placed to bear the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 8:
"Hollow with boards shalt thou make it: as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it." The witnesses note the altar made hollow according to the pattern. On "Hollow with boards shalt thou make it," the grammatical voices note the hollow. Ellicott and Gill note that "hollow with boards shalt thou make it: as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it" — the altar was made hollow (an empty box of boards, not solid), perhaps to be filled with earth or stones, or left hollow for the fire; the hollow altar (light to carry, the boards of acacia overlaid with brass); the figure (some note, the altar hollow, to be filled, perhaps as the cross bears the offering).

On "Hollow with boards shalt thou make it," the witnesses note the structure. Gill notes the altar hollow (boards, not solid, perhaps filled with earth, Exodus 20:24) — the structure; the lightness for carrying.

On "as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the pattern: "as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it" — the altar made according to the pattern shown in the mount (as all the furniture, Exodus 25:40; Hebrews 8:5); the exactness (the altar by the divine model); the providence (the altar a copy of the heavenly pattern, the worship by God's appointment, Hebrews 8:5). The verse displays the altar made hollow according to the pattern.

The thin comment matches the verse. The verse displays the court of the tabernacle (vv.9-19).
9:
"And thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle: for the south side southward there shall be hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side:" The witnesses note the making of the court of the tabernacle. On "thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle," the grammatical voices note the court. Henry and Gill note that "thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle: for the south side... there shall be hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side" — the court (the enclosed outer space around the tabernacle, bounded by hangings of fine twined linen on pillars); the court (the open enclosure, where the altar and laver stood, where the people brought their offerings); the hangings (the white linen walls, a hundred cubits long on the south side).

On "the court of the tabernacle," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the enclosure. Calvin and Ellicott note the court (the enclosure around the tabernacle, the outer area, the place of the altar and laver) — the figure (some note, the court the visible church, or the outer court of God's house, the place of access to the altar).

On "hangings... of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the linen: "hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side" — the white linen hangings (the walls of the court, the figure of purity and righteousness, Revelation 19:8); the enclosure (separating the holy court from the world); the providence (the court the place of approach, the white linen the righteousness required, the figure of the holiness of God's house). The verse displays the making of the court of the tabernacle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the court — "thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle... hangings of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long," the enclosure (Henry, Gill); "the court of the tabernacle," the court (Calvin, Ellicott); "hangings... of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long," the linen (Revelation 19:8, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the court. The verse marks the court: enclosed. The verse displays the hangings: fine linen. The verse displays the enclosure: the walls. The verse displays the pillars and sockets (vv.10-11).
10:
"And the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver." The very brief verse notes the pillars, sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court. The grammatical voices note the materials. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver" — the hangings of the court were hung on twenty pillars (for the south side) set in twenty brazen sockets, with silver hooks and fillets (connecting-rods or bands); the pillars and sockets of brass (the supports of the court); the hooks and fillets of silver (the connecting parts, the silver bands); the structure of the court.

On "the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass," the witnesses note the supports. Gill notes the pillars and sockets of brass (the supports of the hangings, the brazen bases) — the structure; the strength.

On "the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver," the grammatical-historical note the silver: "the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver" — the hooks and fillets of silver (the connecting parts, the silver bands joining the pillars); the gradation of metals (brass for the court, silver for the connections, gold for the holiest); the providence (the court of brass and silver, the gradation of glory from the court to the holiest). The verse displays the pillars, sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 11:
"And likewise for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long, and his twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver." The very brief verse notes the hangings of the north side of the court. The grammatical voices note the side. JFB and Gill note that "likewise for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long, and his twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass; the hooks... and their fillets of silver" — the north side, like the south (vv.9-10), had hangings of a hundred cubits on twenty pillars and twenty brazen sockets, with silver hooks and fillets; the symmetry (the two long sides matching); the enclosure (the white linen walls on the north as on the south).

On "for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long," the witnesses note the symmetry. Gill notes the north side matching the south (the hangings, the pillars, the sockets) — the symmetry; the enclosure.

12:

Gill notes that "for the breadth of the court on the west side shall be hangings of fifty cubits: their pillars ten, and their sockets ten" — the west side (the breadth, the back of the court) had hangings of fifty cubits on ten pillars and ten sockets; the west side (the shorter end, fifty cubits, half the length of the long sides); the enclosure (the white linen on the west, the back of the court closed).

13:
"And the breadth of the court on the east side eastward shall be fifty cubits." The very brief verse notes the breadth of the east side, the entrance. The grammatical voices note the side. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the breadth of the court on the east side eastward shall be fifty cubits" — the east side (the front, the entrance, eastward) was fifty cubits broad (like the west); the east side (the front of the court, where the gate was, vv.16); the entrance (the court entered from the east, the one way in); the orientation (the tabernacle facing east, the entrance toward the sunrise).

On "the breadth of the court on the east side eastward," the witnesses note the front. Gill notes the east side (the front, the entrance) — the orientation; the front of the court.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 14:
"The hangings of one side of the gate shall be fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three." The very brief verse notes the hangings on one side of the gate. 16:
"And for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework: and their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four." The witnesses note the gate of the court — the crux of the one entrance. On "for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen," the grammatical voices note the gate. Ellicott and Gill note that "for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework: and their pillars shall be four" — the gate of the court (the one entrance, twenty cubits wide, in the middle of the east side) was a hanging of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework, on four pillars; the gate (the one entrance into the court, richer than the plain white hangings, the colored gate); the entrance (the one way into the court, where the altar stood).

On the gate, interpreters expound the one entrance. The reformed and the dominant reading note the figure — the gate the one entrance into the court (the only way in, twenty cubits wide, ample, yet one); the witnesses note the lesson — the one gate a figure of Christ the one way to God (the only entrance, "I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved," John 10:9; "I am the way... no man cometh unto the Father, but by me," John 14:6; the one entrance, no other way in; the gate wide enough for all who come, yet the one and only way); the colors (blue, purple, scarlet, white) the figure of Christ (the heavenly, kingly, sacrificial, and pure); the one way of access (through Christ alone, the gate of the court the figure of the way to God).

On "their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the supports: "their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four" — the four pillars of the gate (for the colored hanging); the structure (the gate hung on four pillars); the providence (the one gate, the one way in, the figure of Christ the door). The verse displays the gate of the court.

The witnesses agree the substance: the gate of the court was the one entrance, of rich colors, twenty cubits wide (Ellicott, Gill). 17:
"All the pillars round about the court shall be filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver, and their sockets of brass." The very brief verse notes the silver and brass of the court's pillars. The grammatical voices note the materials. JFB and Gill note that "all the pillars round about the court shall be filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver, and their sockets of brass" — all the pillars of the court were banded (filleted) with silver, with silver hooks, and brass sockets; the silver (the bands and hooks, the connecting silver, the figure of redemption, the silver of the atonement-money, Exodus 30:11-16); the brass (the sockets, the bases, the figure of strength or judgment); the structure of the court.

On "filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver," the witnesses note the silver. Gill notes the silver fillets and hooks (the silver bands joining the pillars) — the connecting silver; the figure (the silver of redemption, the atonement-money, Exodus 30:11-16, binding the court together).

On "their sockets of brass," the grammatical-historical note the brass: "their sockets of brass" — the brazen sockets (the bases of the pillars, standing on brass); the structure (the court resting on brass, joined with silver); the providence (the court of brass and silver, the gradation of metals, the figure of redemption and strength). 18:
"The length of the court shall be an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where, and the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass." The very brief verse notes the dimensions of the court. The grammatical voices note the measures. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "the length of the court shall be an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where, and the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass" — the court was a hundred cubits long, fifty broad, and the hangings five cubits high, of fine twined linen, on brazen sockets; the dimensions (the court a long rectangle, twice as long as broad); the height (the linen walls five cubits high, enclosing the court); the brass sockets (the bases).

On "the length... an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where," the witnesses note the proportions. Gill notes the hundred by fifty cubits (the court's proportions) — the rectangle; the enclosure.

The verse displays the dimensions of the court.

20:
"And thou shalt command the children of Israel, that they bring thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamp to burn always." The witnesses note the pure oil for the light that must burn always — the crux of the continual light. On "thou shalt command the children of Israel, that they bring thee pure oil olive beaten for the light," interpreters expound the oil. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the oil — "pure oil olive beaten for the light": the people were to bring pure olive oil, beaten (pressed from the olives, the finest, clearest oil), for the light of the lampstand; the witnesses note the figure — the pure beaten oil (the finest, the purest, for the holy light); the oil a figure of the Spirit (the oil of the Spirit, the anointing, that feeds the light of testimony and grace, Zechariah 4:2-6, "not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit"; 1 John 2:20, 27, the anointing); the beaten oil (the finest, pressed; some note, the oil pressed, as Christ was bruised, or the saints tried, that the light might burn; the costly purity of the oil for the holy light).

On "to cause the lamp to burn always," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the continual light. Wesley and Henry note "to cause the lamp to burn always" — the lamp to burn continually (always, every night, perpetually, the light never going out, Leviticus 24:2-4; the continual light of the sanctuary); the figure (the light of testimony, the church, the word, ever burning, never extinguished, Matthew 5:14-16; the lamp of God, 1 Samuel 3:3; the light that shines in the darkness, John 1:5; the perpetual witness).

On the light, the grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: the continual light — the pure oil feeding the perpetual lamp; the providence (the light of the sanctuary ever burning, the figure of the Spirit's light and the perpetual testimony of the church, fed by the pure oil of the Spirit). The verse displays the pure oil for the light that must burn always.

The verse displays the tending of the lamp (v.21).
21:
"In the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD: it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations on the behalf of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the priests tending the lamp before the LORD continually. On "In the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony," the grammatical voices note the place. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD" — in the holy place (outside the veil, before the ark of the testimony), Aaron and his sons were to tend (order, trim, keep) the lamp from evening to morning before the LORD; the place (the holy place, where the lampstand stood); the priestly office (the priests tending the light, the daily service); the continual light (kept burning through the night, before the LORD).

On "Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD," the reformed and witnesses note the tending. Gill notes the priests ordering the lamp (trimming, dressing, keeping it burning, evening to morning) — the priestly service; the daily tending of the light (the figure, the ministers keeping the light of testimony burning, the church's light tended).

On "it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations," the wesleyan-arminian Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: "it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations on the behalf of the children of Israel" — the perpetual statute (the tending of the light a lasting ordinance, for all generations); the figure (the perpetual light, the perpetual ministry, the light of testimony kept burning in every generation); the providence (the continual light of the sanctuary, the perpetual ordinance, the figure of the unceasing light of the church and the Spirit, Revelation 1:20; the light ever before God on behalf of the people). The verse displays the priests tending the lamp before the LORD continually.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tending — "Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD: it shall be a statute for ever," the priestly service (Keil, Gill); "Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD," the tending (Gill); "it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations," the perpetuity (Revelation 1:20, Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the tended light. The verse marks the place: before the testimony. The verse displays the priests: ordering the lamp. The verse displays the tending: evening to morning. The verse displays the statute: for ever. The tending displays the priests keeping the light burning. The verse displays the priests: tending. The verse displays the light: continual. The verse displays the ordinance: perpetual. The priests tending the lamp before the LORD continually display the perpetual light: Aaron and his sons order it from evening to morning, a statute for ever (Revelation 1:20) — the priests keeping the light of the sanctuary burning before the LORD, the perpetual ordinance, the figure of the unceasing light of the church. The verse closes the chapter, before the priestly garments (ch.28).
